summary: Gruber responds to a Firefox marketing executive's dismissal of Safari as 'just plain bad' by making a detailed case for Safari as a terrific Mac browser. He argues Safari excels at Apple's institutional priorities — privacy, energy efficiency, and native Mac integration — while Chrome excels at Google's priorities of speed, web-centric computing, and cross-platform reach. He presents battery life data showing Safari lasted 1.5 times longer than Chrome in his own testing on a MacBook Pro. Rather than declaring a winner, he frames the Safari vs. Chrome choice as a matter of ecosystem preference and usage patterns, and expresses gratitude that Mac users have two excellent browser options after years of having none.
key_points: Safari is the only browser that behaves like a true native Mac app, reflecting Apple's priorities of privacy, energy efficiency, and platform integration
Gruber's battery testing showed Safari lasted roughly 1.5x longer than Chrome on the same MacBook Pro running the same test — a difference of nearly two hours
The Safari vs. Chrome choice largely comes down to ecosystem investment: iCloud vs. Google services for syncing tabs, bookmarks, and history
Among DF's Mac readers, 69% use Safari and 28% use Chrome, with Firefox at just 3%
Both browsers are excellent, and the competition between them benefits users — a welcome change from the era when Macs had no good browsers at all
money_quotes: Safari closely reflects Apple's institutional priorities (privacy, energy efficiency, the niceness of the native UI, support for MacOS and iCloud technologies) and Chrome closely reflects Google's priorities (speed, convenience, a web-centric rather than native-app-centric concept of desktop computing, integration with Google web properties).
Safari is Apple's browser for Apple devices. Chrome is Google's browser for all devices.
If you use a Mac laptop, using Chrome instead of Safari can cost you an hour or more of battery life per day.
On the exact same machine running the exact same test with the exact same list of URLs, the battery lasted almost exactly 1.5 times as long using Safari than Chrome.

As someone who's been a Mac user long enough to remember when there were no good web browsers for the Mac, having both Safari and Chrome feels downright bountiful, and the competition is making both of them better.
I really doubt the marketing managers for Chrome or Safari spend their days with a rival browser open for 'play', and even if they did, I expect they'd have the common sense not to admit so publicly.
key_insight: Safari and Chrome each faithfully embody their parent company's values — Apple's native-first privacy focus versus Google's web-centric cross-platform reach — and the real choice between them is which ecosystem and priorities align with your own.
